result:
# The Coin Collector

My grandfather collected coins in blue Whitman folders with circular cutouts, each denomination and year labeled in thin white print. As a child, I'd flip through them on his kitchen table while he made instant coffee, the folder pages stiff as cardboard, some holes filled with dull pennies or silvery dimes, others empty and waiting.

"Still looking for a 1909-S V.D.B.," he'd say, stirring his coffee. "Been looking thirty years."

I never knew what that meant, just that some absences mattered more than others.

---

I think about those folders now when I scroll through my phone at 2 AM, thumb moving in that automatic pattern—refresh, scroll, refresh—looking for something to fill a space I can't quite name. The blue glow makes my bedroom feel underwater. Outside, a car passes, headlights sweeping across my ceiling like a lighthouse, then gone.

There's a message from my sister: "Mom's birthday next week. We should call together."

I star it to respond later. Later becomes never becomes guilt becomes another thing I scroll past.

---

My grandfather died when I was seventeen. At the funeral, my uncle mentioned the coin collection. "Must be worth something," he said, standing by the cheese cubes, doing that thing people do where they negotiate inheritance while pretending they're not.

My grandmother gave it to me instead. "He'd want you to have it."

I thanked her and put the folders in my closet. I didn't look at them for years. What do you do with someone else's incomplete collection? All those gaps felt too intimate, like reading a diary.

---

There's a theory that we're lonelier now than humans have ever been. I read it in an article—one of hundreds I've half-read, tabs open like tiny graves for my attention. The article said something about how we're connected but not *connected*, how we broadcast but don't converse, how we're all standing in crowded rooms shouting into our own phones.

I thought about sharing it but didn't know who to send it to. The irony wasn't lost on me.

---

Yesterday I walked to the coffee shop on Maple Street, the one with the succulents in the window and the barista with the neck tattoo. I ordered my usual—oat milk latte, extra shot—and sat by the window. A woman at the next table was reading an actual book, paper and everything, her finger tracing lines as she went.

I wanted to ask what she was reading. The wanting felt like a small animal in my chest, warm and alive and terrified.

Instead, I opened my phone and pretended to be needed elsewhere.

---

My therapist asks me about connection. "What does it look like for you?"

I tell her about the coins, which surprises us both. I tell her how each one in those folders was found somewhere—in change from a grocery store, in a fountain, in someone else's collection traded at a coin show. How my grandfather must have examined thousands of pennies, hoping each time to find that specific absence filled.

"That sounds patient," she says.

"Or obsessive."

"Maybe both."

---

I've started taking different routes when I walk. This feels worth mentioning. Instead of the direct path to wherever I'm going, I turn down streets I don't know, past houses with gardens I've never seen, where people live entire lives I'll never touch. An old man waters his roses. A kid draws with chalk. A couple argues on a porch, their voices rising and falling like music.

I'm not in those moments, just passing them, but passing feels like something. Feels like collecting, maybe.

---

Last week I finally opened the folders. The coins are still there, still incomplete. The 1909-S V.D.B. penny space still empty. I googled it—it's worth between $700 and $1,500 depending on condition. Not a fortune. Not nothing.

I could buy one, probably. Fill that space he never filled.

But that feels wrong, like finishing someone else's sentence when you didn't hear what they were saying, when you don't know where they were going with it.

---

I called my sister. We talked about Mom's birthday, then about nothing—her job, my apartment, the weird weather, whether we're too old to still not know what we're doing. An hour disappeared. When we hung up, my apartment felt less quiet.

Small thing. Worth mentioning.

---

There's a coin shop downtown I pass sometimes. It has a dusty window display with Morgan silver dollars and Indian head pennies arranged on blue velvet. I've never gone in. Maybe I will. Maybe I'll ask the person behind the counter about my grandfather's collection, about the missing penny, about whether completing someone else's search means anything or changes anything.

Maybe I'll just look, the way my grandfather must have looked, fingers sorting through possibilities, patient with the gaps, understanding that the searching might be the point, that we collect what we can and live with the absences, that sometimes holding the incomplete thing is enough.

The folders are on my kitchen table now. I walk past them every morning.

Still there. Still waiting.

Still mine.
